name: Transaction Classification
description: RECOMMENDED APPROACH A — OWNER-APPROVED DETERMINISTIC RULEBOOK. CLASSIFY queries eligible canonical transactions and active Spend Categories only through the versioned public LEDGER contract. A bounded, versioned normalization step evaluates explicit ordered rules and returns one structured outcome per transaction: deterministic suggestion with match evidence, no suggestion, or conflict. A suggestion never mutates LEDGER. An explicit apply operation uses LEDGER category assignment/correction; OQ-CLASSIFY-4 must decide whether an owner-approved exact rule may batch-apply; if allowed, it occurs only during an explicit invocation with preview evidence, never by a watcher. Corrections remain attributable and may propose a rule change, but do not silently broaden one. Complexity M and within budget: one in-process CLI module, at most one owner-only module store for rule/version/evaluation state, no service or network. Root-problem completeness 9/10. It builds directly on FR-LEDGER-ACTUALS-QUERY, FR-LEDGER-CATEGORY-ASSIGNMENT, FR-LEDGER-CATEGORY-CATALOGUE, FR-LEDGER-CONTRACT-DISCOVERY, and FR-LEDGER-STRUCTURED-INVOCATION while honoring LEDGER NG2 and INGEST NG2. Pros: deterministic, explainable, replayable, correction-aware, and safe for agent use. Cons: normalization and rule lifecycle require discipline; complex merchants can create rule proliferation. Biggest risk: brittle rules can match plausible but wrong transactions after bank-description drift. Stress test: representative held-out transactions must show a material reduction in owner decisions with zero incorrect batch-applied assignments; otherwise stop custom classification and use the do-less path.

ALTERNATIVE B — LOCAL STATISTICAL CLASSIFIER. Train an in-process model from attributable LEDGER assignment history and emit probabilities plus feature explanations; only explicit application mutates LEDGER. Complexity H, beyond the initial M budget, root-problem completeness 8/10, and it still builds on public LEDGER contracts. Pros: can generalize beyond exact recurring descriptions and reduce rule maintenance. Cons: requires enough representative labeled history, model/version lifecycle, evaluation sets, threshold calibration, and explanations that remain meaningful after category or bank drift. Biggest risk: sparse private history produces confident-looking but unstable predictions whose errors contaminate BUDGET and INSIGHTS. Rejected for v1; discovery may preserve it only as a future option if deterministic rules demonstrably plateau and sufficient local labels exist.

ALTERNATIVE C — DO LESS WITH AGENT-GUIDED MANUAL BATCH ASSIGNMENT. Add no classifier state. The AI Agent Host queries uncategorized transactions and active categories through LEDGER, groups likely repeats conversationally, and submits only owner-confirmed assignments through existing LEDGER operations. Complexity L, within budget, root-problem completeness 5/10, and maximal reuse of the current public contract. Pros: little new code, every decision remains explicit, and it is viable immediately after LEDGER. Cons: repeated interpretation remains manual, corrections do not become durable reusable behavior, and results can vary by agent session. Biggest risk: the owner abandons regular categorization as statement volume grows. This is the fallback and kill-criterion outcome, not a straw man.

SELECTION. Choose Approach A because the durable product is not an opaque prediction engine; it is a versioned compilation of owner decisions into reviewable rules and evidence. Keep C as the stop-safe fallback. Do not adopt B unless local evidence later justifies its extra lifecycle and risk. The winner fails if deterministic evidence cannot reduce manual decisions without wrong assignments, if the public LEDGER contract is insufficient, or if useful behavior requires cloud processing.

BOUNDARY SUMMARY. CLASSIFY owns rule definitions, rule versions, suggestion/evaluation outcomes, conflicts, and correction feedback. LEDGER remains authoritative for transaction facts, relationship state, categories, assignments, corrections, actuals, and history. INGEST ends after canonical commit and may only request downstream classification explicitly. BUDGET and INSIGHTS consume LEDGER actuals; CLASSIFY never calculates budgets or reports. CHANNELS owns screens and conversation. Transfers, refunds, voids, supersessions, and archived categories are consumed as LEDGER state, not re-inferred.

SCOPE SIGNALS AND ROUTING. Signals present: sensitive local financial descriptions and rule artifacts; an external AI Agent Host/public LEDGER integration; a likely classification model of at least rules, versions, match evidence, suggestions, feedback, and evaluation runs; a cross-module LEDGER-to-CLASSIFY-to-LEDGER flow consumed by BUDGET/INSIGHTS; and significant unknowns around representative volume, safe features, rule conflicts, approval autonomy, and useful coverage. Five signals are present, with security and significant unknowns independently high-impact. Route to /lex:discovery CLASSIFY before PRD. No new project-wide ADR is required by the selected in-process/public-contract shape; a future statistical model or cloud processing decision would require reassessment and possibly /lex:adr.

DISCOVERY v0.2 — ACTORS, JOB, AND WORKFLOW. Reused project persona P1 Personal Finance Owner; no additional human persona is justified for the sole-owner first release. Job to be done: when canonical transactions arrive, evaluate each category decision once, understand the evidence, and reuse safe owner-approved behavior so recurring bookkeeping becomes consistent without corrupting Ledger facts. Current state: the owner imports or records transactions, queries uncategorized items, repeatedly interprets noisy bank descriptions, assigns categories individually, and later repeats the same judgement. Pain concentrates in repeated interpretation, inconsistent decisions, no reusable correction feedback, and incomplete downstream actuals. Desired state: explicit evaluation produces one suggestion, no-suggestion, or conflict per eligible transaction; the owner can inspect evidence; only an explicit current apply uses public LEDGER operations; rule changes are privately validated and explicitly activated; corrections propose bounded changes; and the AI Agent Host invokes the same versioned contract. UC-CLASSIFY-001 through UC-CLASSIFY-006 capture evaluation, explanation, safe apply, rule lifecycle, correction feedback, and agent discovery.

PRE-MORTEM. Failure 1: description drift or overlapping rules causes plausible wrong categories to be applied at scale, making BUDGET and INSIGHTS confidently wrong; covered by RISK-CLASSIFY-001, -002, and -003 plus zero-error canaries and fail-closed conflicts. Failure 2: recurring decisions are too sparse or rule maintenance costs more than manual LEDGER assignment, so the owner abandons the workflow; covered by RISK-CLASSIFY-005, A1, A3, and the timed kill criteria. Failure 3: descriptions, rules, normalized tokens, or private evidence leak through tracked artifacts, diagnostics, or an agent boundary; covered by RISK-CLASSIFY-004, C5, C9, and EXT-CLASSIFY-HOST-OS-SECURITY.

DEEP INVESTIGATION — CLASSIFICATION SEMANTICS AND RULE SAFETY. The safe v1 semantics are deterministic, read-only evaluation over a versioned allowlist and normalization. One compatible match or multiple matches proposing the same active category may produce one suggestion with all relevant evidence. Incompatible category matches are a Rule Conflict; no implicit priority chooses a winner. No match is an explicit no-suggestion. Suggestion alone never mutates. Concrete fields and predicate grammar remain gated by OQ-CLASSIFY-2 and -3 because the current LEDGER actuals contract names ActualsItem but does not yet define a classification-safe descriptive projection. Pass example: one eligible active transaction matches an active owner-approved rule whose category remains active, so a current suggestion may be explicitly applied. Counterexample: two active rules match the same normalized merchant evidence but propose different categories, so the result is conflict and cannot apply.

DEEP INVESTIGATION — DATA LIFECYCLE AND FEEDBACK. CLASSIFY needs version-addressable rules and rule sets, evaluation fingerprints and outcomes, owner feedback, activation evidence, and private evaluation-run metadata. Ledger remains authoritative for immutable transaction facts, append-only allocation/correction history, relationship/lifecycle state, categories, and idempotent mutations. A correction is feedback against exact provenance and may propose retire, narrow, or replacement behavior; it never generalizes or activates automatically. Category rename preserves the stable identifier; archive/reactivation, transaction void/supersession, relationship or allocation changes, contract/store generation or snapshot changes, and normalization or rule-set changes invalidate affected outcomes. Stale example: a category is archived after preview, so apply is rejected and re-evaluation is required.

DEEP INVESTIGATION — INTEGRATIONS. EXT-CLASSIFY-LEDGER-PUBLIC-CONTRACT is a hard outbound API gate for contract discovery, the classification-safe snapshot projection, active categories, history, assignment/correction, and replay. EXT-CLASSIFY-AI-AGENT-HOST is the inbound owner-context client. EXT-CLASSIFY-HOST-OS-SECURITY provides local identity and storage protection. EXT-CLASSIFY-PRIVATE-EVALUATION-CORPUS provides private representative and held-out evidence. Validation order is contract compatibility, source or client conformance, fingerprint validation, read-only preview, then explicit mutation. The Ledger/Ingest versioned operation registry, one structured stdout result, stable error/exit categories, metadata-only stderr, and idempotency are accepted analogous prior art. Actions HTTP, Web, EF, controller, tenancy, and hosted-service patterns are rejected as inapplicable to this local CLI module.

DEEP INVESTIGATION — SECURITY, PRIVACY, AND EVALUATION. Selective STRIDE disposition: spoofing and elevation are bounded by A6 and the invoking owner OS identity; any multi-user or remote service invalidates that boundary. Tampering is mitigated by immutable version identities and C10 fingerprint-bound activation/apply. Repudiation is mitigated by attributable owner commands, rule provenance, evaluation reports, and Ledger history. Information disclosure is high relevance: descriptions, normalized tokens, financial values, rules derived from spending, and raw corpus rows remain owner-only, untracked, and absent from diagnostics. Denial/resource abuse is bounded by C11 deterministic limits and no-partial-mutation failure. No network or cloud inference is permitted. Compliance disposition is CHECK: household-only personal use introduces no identified regulated processor or hosted retention duty beyond local financial-data protection; adding cloud processing, third-party data handling, shared users, or a service reopens compliance analysis. The supplied PDFs establish that private source evidence exists, but they are not a canonical 90-day LEDGER sample. OQ-CLASSIFY-1, -2, -3, and -7 therefore route to /lex:spike without exposing raw values in the graph.

DOMAIN DISPOSITIONS. Authentication and authorization — CHECK: one trusted local owner identity; no application login or roles in v1, and A6 invalidation reopens this area. Data model and persistence — DEEP: rules, immutable versions, evaluation fingerprints/outcomes, feedback, activation evidence, retention, and cleanup require design. UI and navigation — N/A: CHANNELS owns screens and conversation; CLASSIFY exposes structured CLI operations. Integrations — DEEP: public LEDGER, Agent Host, Host OS security, and private evidence have explicit validation gates. Background and asynchronous processing — N/A: explicit invocation only; no watcher, daemon, scheduler, or implicit INGEST trigger. Reporting and analytics — CHECK: only classification coverage, conflict, drift, false-apply, and time-saved evidence; budgets and financial insights are out of scope. Notifications — N/A: structured results are returned to the invoker; no alert channel is owned here. Configuration and administration — DEEP: rule draft/activation/retirement, allowed predicates, category invalidation, evidence policy, and limits are owner-managed. Multi-tenancy and isolation — N/A under A6: one household owner and OS boundary; any second user invalidates the assumption. Classification semantics and safety — DEEP: eligibility, exact outcome accounting, conflicts, staleness, preview, and no-silent-mutation invariants govern the PRD. Local security and privacy — DEEP: owner-only permissions, payload-free diagnostics, untracked private evidence, integrity fingerprints, and cleanup require acceptance criteria. Compliance — CHECK with no current regulated or hosted scope; reassess on external processing.

GOAL FEASIBILITY AND EVIDENCE. G1 is medium-high confidence analogous to LEDGER snapshot queries, but the classification projection is not yet proven and is gated by A2/OQ-CLASSIFY-2. G2 is low confidence and assumption-based until A1 and the private evidence spike prove useful coverage without false apply. G3 is medium-high confidence analogous to Ledger assignment and idempotency, contingent on published runtime conformance. G4 is medium confidence and assumption-based because useful narrow feedback must be proven on representative corrections. G5 is high confidence analogous to Ledger and Ingest operation registries. G6 is medium-low confidence and assumption-based until the owner-only corpus and temporal hold-out procedure are exercised. No goal depends on a remote service.

KILL-CRITERION AUDIT. The recurrence/manual-time criterion is not triggered but cannot yet be measured; OQ-CLASSIFY-1 and A1 require a private post-INGEST timed spike before implementation planning. The 50-percent reduction and zero incorrect batch-apply criterion is not triggered but cannot yet be measured; OQ-CLASSIFY-7 and A5 gate any broad apply. The public LEDGER sufficiency criterion is not triggered because the graph defines snapshot queries, categories, assignment/correction, discovery, and structured invocation, but its classification-safe projection is incomplete; A2/OQ-CLASSIFY-2 must either close the public gap or route work back to LEDGER before CLASSIFY implementation. The data-locality criterion is not triggered: the selected approach is in-process, owner-only, and network-free. All four remain monitored.

DISCOVERY HANDOFF. The PRD may proceed with suggestion-only default behavior, explicit owner-approved exact-rule batch authority, fail-closed conflicts, fingerprint-bound staleness, private validation, and the public-contract boundary as requirements. Before implementation planning, run the private recurrence/timing and evaluation-corpus spikes. Before technical design fixes a predicate grammar, resolve the classification-safe LEDGER projection and normalization spike. If those results trip a kill criterion, choose the do-less agent-guided LEDGER workflow rather than weakening safety.

DISCOVERY TRACEABILITY AND CROSS-AREA INTERACTIONS. P1 is the primary actor for every CLASSIFY use case. G1 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-001. G2 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-001, UC-CLASSIFY-002, and UC-CLASSIFY-004. G3 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-003. G4 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-005. G5 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-006. G6 maps to UC-CLASSIFY-004. Cross-area fail-closed rules: loss of the trusted owner OS boundary blocks all reads and writes; an unavailable private corpus blocks rule activation but does not rewrite the last active rule set; a category or transaction lifecycle change, correction, relationship change, rule retirement, normalization change, or contract/snapshot mismatch stales pending preview and blocks apply; snapshot expiry or a resource limit aborts evaluation without partial mutation; and an interrupted apply is reconciled only through Ledger idempotency and per-transaction results.
problem_statement: Surface pain: transaction descriptions vary by bank and period, so assigning every transaction manually will not scale and inconsistent assignments weaken budget analysis. 5 Whys: Why classify? BUDGET and INSIGHTS need stable category membership, while LEDGER correctly keeps uncategorized spend explicit. Why is manual assignment insufficient? INGEST will increase the number and recurrence of canonical transactions, and the owner must repeatedly interpret noisy descriptions. Why not let an agent simply guess? Unrecorded guesses are inconsistent, cannot explain confidence, and do not reuse corrections safely. Why do corrections not solve the next transaction today? LEDGER preserves attributable assignments but CLASSIFY has no deterministic feedback contract that turns an owner decision into reusable evidence. Root problem: Tally lacks an auditable, local feedback loop that converts canonical transaction evidence and prior owner-approved decisions into deterministic category suggestions and explicit assignments without rewriting source facts or silently distorting actuals. Without CLASSIFY, the owner repeats the same judgement, uncategorized spend accumulates, and downstream budget and insight results remain incomplete. The sole Personal Finance Owner discovers a versioned CLASSIFY command through the AI Agent Host, evaluates uncategorized canonical transactions, reviews rule evidence or an explicit no-suggestion/conflict result, applies accepted decisions through public LEDGER operations, and uses later corrections to improve future rule proposals.
why_now: INGEST is now fully specified to create canonical transactions at statement scale, while LEDGER already owns category catalogue and attributable assignment/correction. CLASSIFY must define the safe post-commit feedback boundary before BUDGET and INSIGHTS rely on category coverage.

rationale: Classification evaluates and updates canonical ledger transactions and spend-category assignments.
